result:
If I could change the world in one way, it would be to dissolve the illusion of separateness.

I wouldn't change a law, invent a technology, or redistribute resources—though those might be downstream effects. Instead, I would shift the foundational perception that we are fundamentally isolated selves in competition with each other and with nature.

The root of so much suffering—greed, conflict, ecological destruction, loneliness—stems from this deep-seated sense of "us vs. them." It’s the mental boundary that allows us to ignore the pain of others, to hoard while others lack, to see the living world as a commodity.

This change wouldn't be a forced unity or erased individuality. It would be the innate, felt understanding that existence is relational and interconnected. Like suddenly being able to perceive a fundamental layer of reality that was always there: that your wellbeing is woven into mine, that the health of the river is the health of the community, that another's dignity is inseparable from your own.

With that perception altered, actions would transform organically. Empathy would cease to be an effort and become a reflex. Systems of economics, justice, and governance would naturally evolve toward cooperation and sustainability, because harming the whole would be felt as harming oneself.

It’s the deepest leverage point: to change not what people *do*, but what they *perceive* they *are*—from separate cells to inseparable parts of a living whole.
